reviewer: solo new-family build 2026-07-21: educational-quality regression scoring proven FIRST in headless Chrome (transformers.js AutoModel WASM q8), then the deployed page driven end-to-end. Educational passages score markedly higher than casual/promotional text. No media — all input is text; samples are first-party passages.

rubric.evidence: Correctness verified in headless Chrome (transformers.js AutoModel + AutoTokenizer, WASM q8) BEFORE building. The FineWeb-Edu regression head clearly discriminates: a science passage 3.59, a history passage 3.66, a code tutorial 2.75 — vs casual chat -0.22 and promotional spam -0.23 (raw regression, clamped to 0-5). Deployed page (scripts/validate-educational-quality-scorer.mjs 10/10) reproduces this (pre-filled science 3.6; the spam chip drops it to 0.0). The score is the model's real regression output, not canned.
rubric.notes: DISTINCT from every built text classifier: it scores CONTENT QUALITY / educational usefulness, a capability with no built counterpart (sentiment/emotion/toxicity/spam/formality/topic/… are categories, not a quality score).

rubric.evidence: Input a passage → a single REGRESSION score (educational value), clamped to 0-5. A distinct I/O from the built text classifiers (which return class probabilities): this is a scalar quality estimate. Because problem_type=regression / num_labels=1, we run AutoModel and read logits[0] rather than the text-classification pipeline (which would sigmoid it).
rubric.notes: The page maps the score to a 0-5 band with a human-readable description.

rubric.evidence: At-a-glance declares the model (onnx-community/fineweb-edu-classifier-ONNX, linked, ONNX build of HuggingFaceFW/fineweb-edu-classifier), task (text-classification with a REGRESSION head), output (0-5 score), architecture (BERT/arctic-embed + linear regressor), quantisation (q8), backend (WASM), download (~110 MB) and license (Apache-2.0). The How-it-works block shows the real AutoModel call. No invented API.
rubric.notes: It honestly explains WHY AutoModel is used instead of the pipeline (regression head).

rubric.evidence: Loads through the shared createModelLoader auto-init architecture (model {modelId:'onnx-community/fineweb-edu-classifier-ONNX', runtime:'transformers.js', dtype:'q8', sizeMB:110}). First visit shows an explicit Download (~110 MB); the ONNX + tokenizer are cached by transformers.js so a returning visit auto-initialises (checking -> ready) with NO Download click. Verified: fresh profile showed Download; clicking it enabled the editor.
rubric.notes: AutoModel/AutoTokenizer cache under the repo path exactly like a pipeline, so auto-init on return holds via the shared loader.

rubric.evidence: checking -> Download (~110 MB) -> ready (editor enabled, pre-filled sample auto-scored) -> live re-scoring debounced 350 ms after typing. Scoring failures surface on the page in a role=status .err region, not only the console. Verified download -> ready -> score end-to-end.
rubric.notes: Empty input clears the gauge + readout.

rubric.evidence: A real editable textarea (type/paste) plus five sample chips (Science / History / Code tutorial / Casual chat / Spam) that load a passage and re-score. Every control is labelled and keyboard-operable; no dead controls.
rubric.notes: The chips let you see the score swing from ~3.6 (educational) to ~0 (spam) in one click.

rubric.evidence: The gauge + number are the model's real regression output; the readout shows the live backend + latency + the raw (unclamped) score. The page is HONEST: it judges STYLE OF USEFULNESS, not correctness; it's English-web-trained; and the raw value can dip below 0 / above 5 (shown, then clamped to the 0-5 band). Framed as a fast signal, not a verdict.
rubric.notes: Samples are first-party passages whose scores were verified, not asserted.

rubric.evidence: The text box has a visible label; the output region is aria-live; the gauge has role=img with an aria-label stating the score out of 5; the band description is plain text so meaning never depends on the gauge colour. Every chip has an accessible name.
rubric.notes: The numeric score + band text are the accessible source of truth; the coloured gauge is a redundant visual.

rubric.evidence: The textarea, chips, gauge and readout all use fluid widths and wrap; the gauge + ticks are capped and the band text wraps. Verified 0 horizontal overflow at desktop AND mobile 360px (validate 10/10), 0 console errors.
rubric.notes: The gauge max-width keeps it readable without stretching on wide screens.

rubric.evidence: Inference runs in a dedicated module Worker; a single encoder + regression forward pass on a short passage is fast on WASM, and scoring is debounced 350 ms so typing stays smooth. The ~110 MB download is one-time (cached).
rubric.notes: One forward pass yields the score — no autoregression.

guidanceConsulted.query: score text in a worker and keep typing responsive, debounce updates
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Move inference off the main thread; debounce rapid updates so INP stays low.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — scoring runs in a module Worker; live re-scoring is debounced 350 ms.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: typing stays smooth; the editor never blocks on a forward pass

guidanceConsulted.query: accessible score gauge that does not rely on colour alone
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Announce dynamic results in aria-live, give the gauge an accessible name, and provide the value as text.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — the output is aria-live, the gauge is role=img with a spoken score, and the number + band description are plain text.
guidanceConsulted.evidence: score readable as text; the coloured gauge is redundant

guidanceConsulted.query: regression model output vs classification pipeline
guidanceConsulted.recommendation: Use the model head that matches the task; don't force a regression head through a classification pipeline.
guidanceConsulted.appliedOrException: applied — because the head is a regressor (num_labels=1), we run AutoModel and read the raw logit rather than the text-classification pipeline (which sigmoids it into a fake 0-1 probability).
guidanceConsulted.evidence: the displayed score is the true 0-5 regression value

openQuestions: Would a per-sentence heatmap (which lines lift or drop the score) make the signal more actionable for writing feedback?
Is there a cleanly-licensed multilingual educational-quality model, since this one is English-web-trained?
followUpGoals: Add a batch mode: paste many passages and rank them by educational value (its real corpus-curation use).
Add a per-sentence contribution view to show what's driving the score.
Explore a multilingual quality scorer to widen beyond English.
summary: Educational-quality scorer (FineWeb-Edu classifier) — a distinct text family: a 0-5 REGRESSION score of how much a passage teaches, the model HuggingFace used to curate its FineWeb-Edu pre-training corpus. Distinct from every built text classifier (which return categories) — it scores content QUALITY, with no built counterpart. Proven correct in the browser on transformers.js WASM q8 (science/history/tutorial ~3+, casual/spam ~0). ~110 MB, Apache-2.0, no media. A debounced text box + sample chips + a 0-5 gauge; index demo complete + verified 10/10. Honest that it judges style-of-usefulness not correctness and is English-trained. Batch ranking, a per-sentence view, and a multilingual scorer are recorded follow-ups.
